title: Stockholders’ Unrealized Returns and the Market Reaction to Financial Disclosures
abstract: Using both investor- and stock-level data, I examine the relation between stockholders’ unrealized returns since purchase and the market response to earnings announcements. I demonstrate that stockholders’ unrealized gain/loss position moderates their trading behavior in response to earnings announcements. I also find that this behavior generates a short-window return underreaction to earnings news. My results are generally consistent with predictions from prospect theory regarding the manner in which stockholders’ unrealized returns moderate their trading response to belief shocks. However, my results also suggest that an emotional component (i.e., regret-avoidance/pride-seeking) is necessary to explain the observed investor behavior.

article.introduction:
Most economic models of investorbehavior, both rational and behavioral, assume that investors develop beliefs about the uncertain future value of an investment and translate those beliefs into trading decisions using some type of preference (i.e., utility) function. Following this paradigm, a longstanding stream of empirical research shows that investors’ trading decisions vary with their unrealized returns since purchase (hereafter, “unrealized returns”) on a given investment. Specifically, the disposition effect, defined as investors’ tendency to sell winners and hold losers (Shefrin and Statman (1985)), is one of the most widely documented empirical regularities in research that examines individual investor behavior (Barber and Odean (2013)).1The disposition effect is often cited as evidence that investors have utility or risk preferences that depend on whether they are in a cognitive gain or loss frame of reference.2
Recently, however, preference-based explanations for the disposition effect have been called into question. While early studies measured the disposition effect in a binary gain/loss manner (e.g., Odean (1998)), recent studies examine how investors’ likelihood of sale varies with the level of their unrealized returns (Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), Barber and Odean (2013), An (2016)). These studies find that investors’ selling propensity is actually a V-shaped function of unrealized returns: selling probability increases as the magnitude of either gains or losses increases, with the gain side having a steeper slope than the loss side. This empirical evidence is difficult to reconcile with preference-based explanations for the disposition effect. Accordingly, Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012) and An (2016) suggest that, instead of a preference-based explanation, speculative trading (i.e., trading based upon beliefs) may explain the disposition effect observed in prior studies. Specifically, Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012) suggest that rather than stockholders’ preferences moderating their trading decisions in response to individual shocks, stockholders’ unrealized returns may simply be correlated with the arrival of news events that trigger belief-based trading as unrealized returns move away from zero.
Importantly, Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012, p. 2515) note that while a preference-based explanation for the disposition effect implies that “other things equal, having a loss rather than a gain reduces the probability that the stock will be sold[,] … tests of the disposition effect donothold constant other factors influencing selling decisions that may be correlated with profit” [emphasis in original]. The authors go on to conclude that because no attempt is made to control for investors’ beliefs, inferences about how investors’ preferences affect their trading decisions “cannot be drawn from either the disposition effect or our findings” (Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), p. 2522).
In this study, I leverage a controlled setting—the three-day window around earnings announcements—to provide new evidence thatcanbe used to draw inferences as to how investors’ preferences affect their trading decisions. Whereas prior studies examining the disposition effect at the investor level examine the unconditional relation between stockholders’ unrealized returns and all of their selling decisions, I restrict my analysis to stockholders’ trading decisions during three-day windows centered on quarterly earnings announcements. On average, trades occurring during these announcement windows share a common announcement-related trading motivation, and prior research identifies useful measures of the belief shock contained in earnings news. Controlling for the belief shock contained in earnings news as well as other relevant variables allows me to isolate the marginal effect, if any, of stockholders’ unrealized returns on their trading decisions.
Understanding the manner in which stockholders’ unrealized returns moderate their announcement-window trading decisions is important because prior studies predict that preference-driven marginal disposition effects can lead prices to underreact to disclosed information (Frazzini (2006)) and, more generally, can generate return momentum in capital markets (Grinblatt and Han (2005)). Accordingly, I conduct both investor- and stock-level tests to determine whether the behavior observed in my investor-level tests aggregates to affect stock prices.
At the investor level, I analyze the announcement-window trading decisions of institutional fund managers. I obtain data from the commercially available Ancerno Ltd. database, which contains the transaction histories of a sample of institutional investors.3I use Ancerno-provided identity codes to track daily unrealized gains and losses in individual positions taken by these fund managers. Using these data, I study the shape of the marginal relation between the fund's unrealized return in a given stock and the fund manager's decision to sell during the earnings announcement window.4
Following Barber and Odean (2013), I estimate a Cox (1972) proportional hazard rate model to analyze fund managers’ conditional selling probabilities at various levels of returns since purchase. Importantly, my model controls for the earnings surprise as well as a number of additional controls identified in prior literature. I find that, estimated at the quarterly median of earnings news, Ancerno fund managers’ selling schedule reveals a marginal disposition effect that approximates an inverse V-shape, as predicted by recent models based on prospect theory (Kaustia (2010), Meng (2012), Li and Yang (2013)). Relative to the baseline hazard rate estimated for stocks with close to zero returns (−2% to 2%), the hazard that a stock will be sold decreases with both positive and negative returns since purchase, with a steeper decrease in the loss domain. This generates a marginal disposition effect such that, ceteris paribus, the hazard-of-sale for stocks that have increased from 22% to 26% since purchase is approximately 1.24 times the hazard-of-sale of stocks that have decreased from 22% to 26%.
Notably, however, this relation varies with earnings news. In particular, stockholders in a gain position are more likely to sell as earnings news increases, while stockholders in a loss position are less likely to sell as earnings news increases. Given that postannouncement stock prices continue to move in the direction of earnings news (Ball and Brown (1968), Bernard and Thomas (1990)), speculative traders should be less likely to sell as earnings news increases. Thus, the observed behavior is consistent with speculative trading motives only for stockholders in a loss position. In contrast, both sides of the observed interaction are consistent with the framework of Shefrin and Statman (1985) in which the emotional responses of regret-avoidance and pride-seeking contribute to the disposition effect. Specifically, my results are consistent with stockholders relying on earnings news to justify or discipline their tendencies toward pride-seeking and regret-avoidance.5The observed interaction also suggests that, at the stock level, the disposition effect is more likely to generate a return underreaction to good news announcements than bad news announcements.
To assess whether the investor-level behavior I observe aggregates to generate the short-window return underreaction to earnings news predicted by Frazzini (2006), I estimate a stock-level measure of stockholders’ average unrealized capital gain/loss position in a firm's equity (hereafter, stockholders’ capital gains overhang, or CGO), as in Grinblatt and Han (2005). Controlling for earnings news and other known determinants of announcement-window abnormal trading volume, I find that, on average, the relation between stockholders’ CGO and abnormal trading volume is positive, consistent with a marginal disposition effect. A number of additional tests show that this marginal disposition effect is driven by a preference-based explanation. First, the marginal disposition effect is stronger in seller-initiated trades than in buyer-initiated trades. Second, the effect is stronger when investors are less sophisticated (Dhar and Zhu (2006)) and when the firm's information environment is sparse (Kumar (2009)). Third, the marginal disposition effect increases with idiosyncratic return volatility.6
More importantly, I find evidence that this marginal disposition effect generates the short-window underreaction to earnings news predicted by Frazzini (2006). Frazzini (2006) argues that when stockholders have a disposition to sell following good news or to hold following bad news, this temporarily depresses or inflates stock prices, respectively, causing prices to adjust to earnings news more slowly. Consistent with this view, I find that when earnings news and stockholders’ CGO are strongly of the same sign, the announcement-window return sensitivity to earnings news is approximately 36.6% lower, relative to the average return response to earnings news. Following such announcements, I also document a corresponding correction in postannouncement returns, as evidenced by a 60-trading-day abnormal hedge return of approximately 6.38%. Similar to my findings at the investor level, when I examine separate stock-level portfolio sorts for high and low earnings surprise quintiles, return spreads are only statistically significant for high earnings surprise (i.e., good news) quintiles. The observed underreaction to earnings news also varies along the predicted dimensions of investor sophistication, richness of the firm's information environment, and idiosyncratic return volatility.
Taken together, the investor- and stock-level results in this study provide evidence that stockholders’ unrealized returns moderate their trading response to financial disclosures, and that this behavior affects the price response to disclosed information. The results of this paper thus help reconcile experimental evidence of stockholders’ unrealized returns moderating their trading decisions (e.g., Weber and Camerer (1998), Summers and Duxbury (2012)) with recent empirical evidence of a V-shaped unconditional disposition effect (Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), Barber and Odean (2013), An (2016)). I find that the shape of the marginal disposition effect that I observe differs from the shape of the unconditional disposition effect observed in prior studies. This validates concerns raised by Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012) and suggests that future studies testing predictions about stockholders’ preferences should examine the marginal, rather than unconditional, effect of stockholders’ unrealized returns on their selling decisions. My results also extend prior research examining the role of regret-aversion and pride-seeking in explaining stockholders’ trading behavior with respect to their prior returns (Shefrin and Statman (1985), Strahilevitz, Odean and Barber (2011), Summers and Duxbury (2012), Barber and Odean (2013)).
My stock-level results directly extend prior work by Frazzini (2006). Frazzini (2006) documents drift in monthly returns when stockholders’ unrealized capital gains have the same sign as earnings news, and, absent short-window evidence, infers that his results are driven by a marginal announcement-window disposition effect. Recent asset pricing studies, however, question the link between the disposition effect and future stock returns (e.g., Novy-Marx (2012), An (2016)). My stock-level tests contribute to this literature by providing controlled short-window evidence of a marginal association between stockholders’ unrealized returns and the market reaction to earnings news. My results also complement those from prior studies examining investor inattention around earnings announcements (DellaVigna and Pollet (2009), Hirshleifer, Lim, and Teoh (2009)), consistent with the statement of Barber and Odean (2008, p. 785) that “preferences determine [investors’] choices after attention has determined the choice set.”
The remainder of the paper proceeds as follows. SectionIdescribes the data sources and sample selection procedure for the data used in this study. SectionIIreports results of my investor-level tests. SectionIIIprovides results of stock-level tests that examine whether stockholders’ preferences moderate their announcement-window trading decisions, and SectionIVexamines whether this behavior generates an underreaction to earnings news. SectionVdiscusses robustness and concludes.
article.I. Data and Sample Selection:
I examine both investor- and stock-level data around earnings announcements. I obtain investor-level data from the Ancerno Ltd. institutional trading database, which accounts for approximately 8% of CRSP dollar volume (Puckett and Yan (2011)).7This database tracks individual transactions made by large institutional investors who hire Ancerno to assist them in monitoring their trading costs. As described by Puckett and Yan (2011), the database should include the complete transaction history for each investor during the time period over which they contract with Ancerno.
A key feature of the database is that from approximately 2000 to 2010, Ancerno provides a unique (anonymized) identifier for each investor. I use these identifiers to accumulate daily investor-stock share positions over time. I focus on Ancerno investors classified as fund managers, as they account for the majority of trading activity in the database and are more likely to retain trading authority over their funds relative to pension plan sponsors. Similar to Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), I conduct several procedures to collect and clean the data. I provide a detailed description of the data collection process in SectionIof the Internet Appendix. Panel A of TableIsummarizes the sample that I obtain from the Ancerno database. In total, I am able to cleanly track 159,032 unique stock positions taken by Ancerno fund managers during my sample period, which correspond to a total of 70,101,493 investor-stock-days. Some positions are closed during the sample collection period, while others remain open through the end of the sample collection period.
The key motivation underlying my investor-level tests is to analyze stockholders’ selling decisions in a controlled setting, but it is important to note that the fund managers whose behavior I observe in the commercially available Ancerno database differ in a number of respects from the retail investors examined in prior studies (e.g., Odean (1998), Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), Barber and Odean (2013)). There are both advantages and disadvantages to examining the announcement-window decisions of institutional investors versus those of retail investors. Although unconditional disposition effects have been documented among institutional investors in a number of settings around the world, institutional disposition effects have consistently been shown to be weaker than those exhibited by retail investors.8Prior research focusing on institutional investors in the Ancerno database also finds that Ancerno institutions are larger than average 13-F filing institutions and that they execute informed interim trading decisions (Puckett and Yan (2011)).
These findings bias against observing a marginal disposition effect in the announcement-window trading of Ancerno fund managers, even if such an effect might be present in the trading behavior of less sophisticated investors. However, as Jin and Scherbina (2011, p. 791) note because institutional funds “control a large portion of the U.S. stock market, managers’ behavioral biases may have significant asset pricing implications.” Thus, focusing on Ancero fund manager trading behavior versus retail investor behavior is advantageous in that the results of my stock-level tests are more likely to reflect the announcement-window trading decisions of large institutional fund managers relative to those of retail investors. Accordingly, I analyze the trading behavior of Ancerno fund managers in my investor-level tests and then, at the stock level, examine whether the strength of my results varies cross-sectionally with institutional ownership to determine whether a stronger marginal disposition effect is observed among less sophisticated investors.
Specifically, I collect a stock-level sample that combines data from a number of sources and then merge these stock-level data with the Ancerno sample to conduct investor-level tests. My stock-level sample combines accounting data from Compustat, daily stock price and share volume data from CRSP, analyst forecasts and reported actual earnings per share data from I/B/E/S, detailed trade and quote data from the NYSE's Trade and Quote (TAQ) database, and 13-F institutional holdings data from the Thompson Reuters CDA/Spectrum database. Following prior literature (Lee (1992), Bhattacharya (2001)), my study includes TAQ trades with a condition code of “regular sale” between 9:30 a.m. and 4:15 p.m. (EST), excluding each day's opening trade. I collect data for a sample of quarterly earnings announcements for the years 1994 (the first full year for which the required TAQ data are available) through 2010 (the final year of data availability from Ancerno Ltd). I require each firm-quarter observation in the sample to have sufficient data to calculate the variables defined in equations4and5, which results in a sample of 192,918 firm-quarter observations for 8,947 unique firms.
I match the stock-level data to Ancerno investor-stock-days that fall within the (−1,1) three-day announcement window using CRSP PERMNOs.9A unique concern with the Ancerno database is that clients may stop contracting with Ancerno at any point during the sample period, which means that they can disappear from the data without closing their positions. I employ two filters to prevent this from affecting my analysis. First, in the overall sample, I stop tracking all positions for an investor on the last date the investor reports a trade to Ancerno. Second, in the sample that I analyze in my study, I consider only the investor-stock-days on which the investor made a sale in at least one stock in their portfolio, following Odean (1998) and Kaustia (2010). This ensures that the investor was actively contemplating a sale transaction on the date of any observation included in the data.
Consistent with Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), I do not include investor-stock-day observations for a position's initial purchase date, which means I delete round-trip positions opened and closed on the same day. Given these requirements, I am able to match 97,285 (50.4%) of the earnings announcements in the stock-level data set to announcement-window holdings of Ancerno investors. To mitigate the impact of outliers and underlying data errors, I also delete observations for which the magnitude of unexpected earnings is larger than the stock price (i.e., values less than −1 or greater than 1). As shown in Panel A of TableIannouncement-window dates on which an investor makes a sale in at least one investment in her portfolio make up a small proportion of the overall Ancerno sample, resulting in a final announcement-window sample of 1,177,583 unique investor-stock-days.
article.II. Investor-Level Tests:
Barber and Odean (2013) provide a comprehensive review of research examining the disposition effect. They note that “while the tendency of investors to sell winners more readily than losers is empirically robust, recent research focuses on why investors behave this way” (Barber and Odean (2013, p. 1557)).
Shefrin and Statman (1985) motivate the disposition effect using a four-part framework that incorporates aspects of prospect theory (Kahneman and Tversky (1979)), mental accounting (Thaler (1985)), the emotions of regret-aversion and pride-seeking, and self-control issues. The diminishing sensitivity feature of prospect theory is often cited as the key mechanism generating the disposition effect, such that investors in a gain position are risk-averse and inclined to sell, while investors in a loss position are risk-seeking and tend to hold (Li and Yang (2013)).10Recent studies also consider alternative preference functions such as realization utility, which posits that investors exhibit the disposition effect because they derivedirectutility (disutility) fromrealizinggains (losses) (Barberis and Xiong (2012), Ingersoll and Jin (2013)).11In my study I draw on predictions from prospect theory, but it is likely that a realization utility model could generate many of the same predictions, and I do not attempt to differentiate between these two preference-based explanations for the disposition effect. More importantly, these preference-based explanations for the disposition effect suggest that, ceteris paribus, investors’ risk (or realization utility) preferences with respect to their unrealized returns since purchase moderate their trading decisions.
Other recent studies challenge this explanation. Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012) examine retail investors’ likelihood of sale at varying levels of their returns since purchase. They also test for a discontinuity in investors’ selling decisions between stocks with very small losses and stocks with very small gains, to identify whether the disposition effect is driven by a straightforward sign realization preference, such that investors prefer to realize small gains over small losses. Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012) find minimal evidence of this simple form of preference function driving the disposition effect, and instead find that the disposition effect arises from an asymmetric V-shaped function of unrealized returns.
As stated in the introduction, Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012) and An (2016) suggest that, instead of a preference-based explanation, speculative trading may explain the V-shaped disposition effect. They argue that news arrival motivates investors to update their beliefs and transact, and that both positive and negative unrealized returns may be correlated with the arrival of news.12A key takeaway is that studies that examine theunconditionaldisposition effect are confounded and do not provide evidence as to whether stockholders’ returns since purchase moderate their trading decisions, controlling for other trading motivations such as news arrival.
To address this concern, I analyze the announcement-window selling decisions of Ancerno fund managers to provide evidence on whether their unrealized returns moderate their propensity to sell in response to the belief shock contained in earnings news. If fund managers’ announcement-window trading decisions are purely speculative, I should not observe amarginaldisposition effect after controlling for the belief shock contained in earnings news. In this case, I may not observe any incremental relation between fund managers’ returns since purchase and their trading decisions, controlling for earnings news, or I may observe areversedisposition effect if Ancerno fund managers are tax-sensitive in their speculative response to earnings news (Jin (2006)). Either of these results is potentially consistent with the proposed explanation of Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012) for the unconditional V-shaped disposition effect observed in prior studies.
If I do observe a marginal disposition effect in fund managers’ response to earnings news, this would support the notion that stockholders’ preferences with respect to their unrealized returns moderate their trading decisions. Further, the shape of the marginal relation between fund managers’ unrealized returns and their selling decisions may offer insights into the manner in which stockholders’ preferences affect their trading decisions. To motivate my predictions about the shape of this marginal relation, I draw from a recent prospect theory model that highlights conditions under which stockholders’ prospect theory preferences are expected to generate a marginal disposition effect in their trading response to a belief shock.
In SectionIIof the Internet Appendix, I demonstrate that the recent model of Li and Yang (2013) implies aninverseV-shaped pattern in the marginal relation between investors’ propensity to sell in response to a random belief shock and their returns since purchase.13Earlier prospect theory models by Kaustia (2010) and Meng (2012) make similar predictions.14Essentially, this inverted V-shape stems from a combination of two effects, such that the loss aversion aspect of prospect theory predicts that stockholders have a higher probability of selling risky positions when returns are close to zero, while diminishing sensitivity predicts a higher average likelihood of sale in the gain domain of the inverted V-shape than the loss domain. Evidence of this pattern in Ancerno fund managers’ announcement-window trading decisions would lend support to the analytical framework of Li and Yang (2013).
Note that, if I observe a V-shapedmarginaldisposition effect in fund managers’ announcement-window trading decisions, similar to the shape observed for the unconditional disposition effect, this wouldnotlend credence to the speculative trading hypothesis proposed by Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012). The speculative explanation for the unconditional disposition effect observed in prior research is not an “all else equal” argument, but rather relies on a correlation between returns since purchase and information arrival. Thus, if I observe a marginal V-shaped disposition effect, this would suggest one of two possibilities: (a) I have not properly controlled for the correlation between information arrival and belief shocks, or (b) there is an alternate explanation for theunconditionalV-shaped disposition effect observed in prior studies.
Finally, a marginal disposition effect that is motivatedsolelyby stockholders’ risk preferences with respect to their unrealized returns should exhibit a constant shape that does not vary with earnings news. In stylized economic models, such as that analyzed by Li and Yang (2013), it is possible (and often convenient) to orthogonalize the effects of investors’ beliefs and preferences. In reality, however, it is possible that investors’ beliefs and preferences interact to jointly determine their trading decisions. In other words, any marginal disposition effect that I observe may become stronger or weaker depending on earnings news. I allow for this possibility in my empirical design and further discuss its implications when describing my results.
whereh(t,x(t)) is the hazard rate at timetconditional on a set ofpobserved predictors as of periodt(denotedx(t)). The baseline hazard rate,h0(t), is the hazard rate when all predictors take on a value of 0. Theβcoefficients are estimated from the data. The hazard rate is the probability density function of the hazard event at timetconditional on survival to timet, where the hazard event is the sale of a stock and time is measured in days since purchase. The hazard rate for a particular stock being sold by a particular investor is conditional on the covariates for that stock and investor at timet.
The baseline hazard rate is the omitted return category that includes returns from −2% to 2%. The hazard-of-sale ratio, exp(ai), is the ratio of hazard rates between return categoryaiand the baseline hazard, holding all other covariates constant. To develop robust estimates of Ancerno fund managers’ unconditional hazard-of-sale function, I randomly draw 100 samples of data from the full sample of 70,101,493 investor-stock-days, where each sample contains the investor-stock-days for 10,000 randomly drawn round-trip stock positions.16This procedure ensures that each random sample contains stock positions that are cleanly observable from purchase through eventual liquidation, but it allows variable holding periods for each position. The resulting randomly drawn samples contain an average of 1,161,766 investor-stock-days per estimation sample.
Figure1plots the estimated hazard-of-sale ratio (y-axis) for each unrealized return category (x-axis), based on the mean coefficients from the 100 randomly drawn estimation samples. Dotted lines indicate 95% confidence intervals based on mean standard errors. Statistical significance for each return category indicator (Ret_Cati) coefficient (ai) can be assessed visually by noting whether the 95% confidence interval differs from 1.00 on they-axis. As in prior literature, the results show that the tendency to sell a stock increases with both positive and negative returns since purchase, with a disposition effect evidenced by the steeper slope on the positive return side of the V-shape. Consistent with prior studies examining the disposition effect among institutional investors, I find a less dramatic disposition effect among Ancerno fund managers compared to the analysis of Barber and Odean (2013) of retail investors. For example, I find that the hazard-of-sale for stocks up 18% to 22% since purchase is 1.16× greater than the baseline rate and 1.11× greater than the hazard rate for stocks down 18% to 22%, compared to the 2.65× and 1.77×, respectively, reported by Barber and Odean (2013).
whereUEis unexpected earnings, measured as the forecast error relative to I/B/E/S analyst forecasts as in Hirshleifer, Lim, and Teoh (2009). As in Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), I control for the time since the investor opened the position in the stock, the investor's purchase price in the investment, and stock return volatility. Because I analyze the stock-level reaction to earnings news in tests below, I also add relevant controls from the stock-level sample. These controls, which have been shown to affect trading around earnings announcements, include market capitalization, momentum, average turnover in the stock over the previous year, and market turnover during the announcement window. Descriptive statistics for the variables included in my model are presented in Panel C of TableI. Formal definitions for all variables are provided in the Appendix.
I rankUEand all control variables into deciles each quarter, and I scale decile rankings to have a mean of 0 and a range of 1.17Therefore, the baseline hazard rate in my expanded model represents the hazard rate for the omitted return category (−2% to 2%) at the median level of quarterly earnings news and at the medians of the other control variables included in the model.
Panel A of Figure2plots the hazard-of-sale ratio (y-axis) for various levels of unrealized returns (x-axis), at the medians of the other variables included in the model. Dotted lines indicate 95% confidence intervals based on Huber-White robust standard errors. The figure indicates that, ceteris paribus, for returns ≤ −6% or greater than 10%, estimated hazard-of-sale ratios are significantly less than 1.00. Thus, relative to the baseline hazard rate for stocks with close to 0 returns, the hazard that a stock will be sold decreases with both positive and negative returns since purchase, approximating the inverse V-shape predicted by prospect theory.
A steeper downward slope in the loss domain relative to the gain domain generates a marginal disposition effect. The loss domain return groups become significantly less than 1 at a lower absolute magnitude than the gain domain return groups (i.e., 6% vs. 10%), and, for absolute return magnitudes larger than 18%, the hazard ratios in the gain domain are significantly higher than those in the loss domain at the 5% level or better. For example, ceteris paribus, the hazard-of-sale for stocks that have increased 22% to 26% since purchase is approximately 1.24 times the hazard-of-sale of stocks that have decreased 22% to 26% (p< 0.05).
Panel B of Figure2plots the marginal hazard ratio associated with a one-unit increase inUE, at varying levels of unrealized returns, all else equal. A one-unit increase can be interpreted as the difference between low and high quarterlyUEdeciles. Statistical significance for the coefficient onUE(β1), as well as eachbicoefficient, can be assessed visually by noting whether the 95% confidence interval differs from 1.00 on they-axis. When returns are close to 0, the effect ofUEis not statistically significant. However, as unrealized returns become more positive (negative), the effect ofUEbecomes significantly positive (negative), such that thebicoefficients are significantly positive at the 5% level for 9 of 16 return categories in the gain domain and significantly negative for 7 of 11 return categories in the loss domain. For instance, for stocks with unrealized returns from 22% to 26%, the hazard ratio of 1.41 indicates that a one-unit change inUEis associated with a 41% increase in the hazard rate of sale, all else equal (p< 0.01).
Figure3combines both panels of Figure2to depict the interaction between unrealized returns and unexpected earnings. The figure presents estimated conditional hazard ratios comparing stockholders’ hazard-of-sale at various levels of unexpected earnings and return since purchase with the baseline hazard for stocks with unrealized returns of −2% to 2% at the median level of quarterly earnings news. These estimated hazard ratios are presented as a three-dimensional surface where they-axis (x-axis) of each figure denotes values of unexpected earnings (returns since purchase), and estimated hazard ratios are plotted, in color, on thez-axis. Lighter shaded regions correspond to higher estimated selling probabilities, and darker shaded regions correspond to lower estimated selling probabilities, relative to the baseline hazard rate. Panel A presents a “heat map” of the estimated surface, viewed from above, while Panel B offers a three-dimensional view of the same surface from −15° azimuth, 35° elevation. The interaction is evident from the downward slope across unexpected earnings to the left of zero and upward slope across unexpected earnings to the right of zero.
The overall shape of the combined surface exhibits the inverse V-shaped pattern, peaking to the right of zero, predicted by prospect theory. However, if the marginal disposition effects that I observe were driven solely by stockholders’ preferences with respect to their unrealized returns, the shape of the marginal disposition effect would not vary with earnings news. This suggests that an additional mechanism contributes to the manner in which stockholders’ unrealized returns affect their announcement-window trading decisions. Further, given that it is well known that earnings surprises are persistent and stock prices generally drift in the direction of earnings news (Ball and Brown (1968), Bernard and Thomas (1990)), I expect that if the interactions I observe were driven solely by speculative trading motives, stockholders would sell in response to negative earnings surprises and hold in response to positive earnings surprises. However, I observe this negative relation between earnings news and likelihood of sale only for stockholders in a loss position.
Regret is an emotional feeling associated with the ex post knowledge that a different past decision would have fared better than the one chosen. The positive counterpart to regret is pride. While closing a stock account at a loss induces regret, closing at a gain induces pride. The quest for pride, and the avoidance of regret lead to a disposition to realize gains and defer losses.
Consistent with this argument, in recent experimental work Summers and Duxbury (2012) find that disposition effects arise only when subjects feel responsible for the initial investment decision, consistent with the emotions of regret and pride being necessary to generate the disposition effect. In recent archival work, Strahilevitz, Odean, and Barber (2011) document that individual investors are more likely to repurchase a stock that they have previously sold if the price has dropped since their previous transaction. The authors attribute this behavior to the emotions of regret when one repurchases at a higher price than one sold at and pride when one repurchases at a lower price (Barber and Odean (2013)).
The interaction I observe between stockholders’ unrealized returns and unexpected earnings in determining their announcement-window hazard-of-sale is consistent with stockholders relying on earnings news to justify or discipline their tendencies toward pride-seeking and regret-avoidance. For a stockholder in a loss position, an increase in earnings news offers hope that the investment will return to profitability, which justifies the stockholder's desire to hold and potentially avoid the feelings of regret associated with recognizing a loss. For stockholders in a gain position, an increase in earnings news amplifies the stockholder's sense of pride associated with her initial decision to invest in a profitable company (as opposed to experiencing a windfall gain), which amplifies the stockholder's emotional desire to sell.
Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012) note that stockholders’ length of holding period is an important determinant of their selling decisions. My proportional hazards model is robust to stockholders’ baseline hazard-of-sale varying in any manner over their holding period, even nonlinearly. However, as Barber and Odean (2013) note, the model assumes that hazard ratios remain proportional between covariates over time. In other words, my model makes no assumptions about how the unconditional rate of selling stocks changes from day 50 to day 100, but it does assume that if a winner is sold at a 20% higher rate than a loser on day 50, then the winner will also be sold at a 20% higher rate than a loser on day 100 (Barber and Odean (2013)).
While Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012) find that the V-shaped unconditional disposition effect is stronger for shorter holding periods, violations of the proportional hazards assumption do not appear to substantively affect the inferences from the analysis of Barber and Odean (2013): their hazard rate models find consistent evidence of the V-shaped unconditional disposition effect across two independent data sets.
Nevertheless, in Panel B of TableI, I examine various holding-period subsamples to investigate whether the basic gain versus loss announcement-window disposition effect varies with time since purchase in my sample. Consistent with prior research (Dhar and Zhu (2006); Frazzini (2006)), the overall magnitude of the average gain versus loss disposition effect among Ancerno fund managers is smaller than that reported for retail investors (15.58% of the unconditional probability of sale, compared with 44.68% in Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012)).
Within holding-period subsamples, I find puzzling results for observations with short holding periods (1–100 days). Although these observations represent a relatively small proportion of my sample (18%), I find that fund managers exhibit low selling probabilities and a slightreversedisposition effect over short holding periods. While I reliably observe an announcement-window disposition effect at longer holding periods, which leads to an overall disposition effect in my sample, the changing relation between fund managers’ unrealized returns and likelihood of sale suggests a possible violation of the proportional hazards assumption in my data.
Accordingly, in SectionIIIof the Internet Appendix, I perform two sets of analyses to examine this concern further. Similar to the tests in Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), I use local polynomial smoothing to plot the univariate announcement-window relation between investors’ return since purchase and their propensity to sell at various holding periods. I also estimate multivariate probit regressions to examine themarginaldisposition effect over various subsamples, similar to the analysis in Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012). I continue to observe puzzling results at shorter holding periods. Specifically, I observe a V-shaped marginal disposition effect at short holding periods (fewer than 100 days), but an inverse V-shape, consistent with the results of the proportional hazards model, for longer holding periods. The interaction between unrealized returns and unexpected earnings in determining stockholders’ announcement-window selling decisions holds across all holding periods.
The reverse disposition effect that I observe among Ancerno fund managers at short holding periods could be consistent with these fund managers exhibiting tax-rational behavior.18Alternatively, it could reflect measurement error in my research proxies or limitations in my identification strategy. For example, as discussed by Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), the V-shaped relation may arise from a confounding correlation between unrealized returns and news arrival. In this regard, my identification strategy is weakest for short holding periods. When fund managers hold stocks for longer periods, they are more likely to have previously committed to a long-term position in those stocks, and their announcement-window selling decisions are more likely to be motivated by the information contained in the announcement. Further, longer-term unrealized returns are less correlated with events from the current quarter, which are reflected in the earnings surprise, than short-term unrealized returns. Given the puzzling results discussed in this subsection, caution should be observed in applying the investor-level inferences from my study to investors with short trading horizons.
article.III. Stock-Level Tests of Stockholders’:
Prior studies predict that preference-driven marginal disposition effects can cause prices to underreact to disclosed information (Frazzini (2006)) and, more generally, generate return momentum in capital markets (Grinblatt and Han (2005)). In this section, I test for a marginal announcement-window disposition effect at the stock level and for evidence of its associated effect on stock prices. My investor- and stock-level tests complement each other in notable ways. At the investor level, my tests assume that fund managers’ trades in the short windows around earnings announcements are motivated by the announcements. At the stock level, I estimate abnormal trading volume and returns around earnings announcements to better isolate market behavior induced by the announcement.
However, a drawback of this approach is that, at the stock level, I am not able to observe a precise measure of each stockholder's unrealized return. Therefore, to provide evidence of a marginal disposition effect at the stock level, I test for a positive association between stockholders’ CGO and abnormal seller-initiated announcement-window trading volume. Although prior studies (e.g., Lakonishok and Smidt (1986), Statman, Thorley, and Vorkink (2006)) demonstrate evidence ofunconditionalstock-level disposition effects inaveragetrading volume, my tests extend prior literature by testing for amarginaldisposition effect inabnormal seller-initiatedvolume in response to a belief shock, consistent with predictions about the manner in which stockholders’ preference functions moderate their trading decisions.19Given that stockholders’ CGO aggregates varying levels of unrealized returns across stockholders, I make no predictions about the precise shape of the marginal relation between CGO and trading volume or the manner in which this shape varies with earnings news. With that said, Iamable to test cross-sectional predictions as to whether the strength of the positive association between stockholders’ CGO and abnormal trading volume varies with characteristics of the stock and stockholder base (e.g., the percentage of shares held by institutions).
Prior literature also predicts that preference-driven marginal disposition effects can impact abnormal returns around financial disclosures (Frazzini (2006), Li and Yang (2013)). In the model of Li and Yang (2013), traders in the risk-averse unrealized gain portion of their preference function are more likely to sell in response to a belief shock. This increases the supply of shares, and the excess supply suppresses stock prices. Similarly, traders in the convex loss portion of their preference function are less likely to sell absent a price premium. This decreases the supply of shares and inflates current stock prices.20Thus, thepositiveassociation between stockholders’ CGO and abnormalvolumepredicts anegativeassociation between stockholders’ CGO and abnormalreturns.
Prior literature further predicts that if these associations are driven by stockholders’ reference-dependent preferences moderating their selling decisions, then the strength of the observed associations should vary systematically along a number of dimensions. I focus on three empirical predictions discussed in Li and Yang (2013). Specifically, I posit that the disposition effect in abnormal volume and its pricing implication for returns are stronger for stocks with (1) higher expected return volatility, (2) lower expected return skewness, and (3) lower investor sophistication.21
I employ a transaction-based (i.e., number-of-trades-based) measure of abnormal trading volume. Trade type indicates that the measure includes all trades, denoted byAVOLTOTAL TRADES; buyer-initiated trades, denoted byAVOLBUYER-INIT TRADES; or seller-initiated trades, denoted byAVOLSELLER-INIT TRADES.22A transaction-based measure of trading volume is most closely aligned with prior research on the disposition effect. For example, studies documenting the disposition effect at the investor level generally consider whether a sale took place, not the number of shares sold (e.g., Odean (1998), Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012)). If the disposition effect in abnormal trading volume arises from sellers’ preferences, then the positive association between stockholders’ CGO and abnormal volume should be strongest for seller-initiated trades.23I test this prediction in SectionIVof the Internet Appendix and, as predicted, find a stronger association between stockholders’ CGO and seller-initiated abnormal trading volume. Accordingly, in the results presented hereafter, I focus on seller-initiated abnormal volume as the dependent measure of interest.
To test whether these associations vary with the predicted stock and stock-holder characteristics, I interactCGOwith relevant proxies for each prediction. To test the predictions of Li and Yang (2013) about volatility and skewness, I interactCGOwith idiosyncratic return volatility (IVOL) and idiosyncratic return skewness (ISKEW). I predict a positive (negative) coefficient onCGO*IVOLand a negative (positive) coefficient onCGO*ISKEWin model (3) (model (5). I also interactCGOwith two proxies to test whether the marginal associations I observe vary with investor sophistication: analyst following (FOLLOWING) and institutional ownership ratio (IOR).25I predict negative coefficients onCGO*FOLLOWINGandCGO*IORin model (4) and positive coefficients in model (5).
In each model, I include controls for variables that have been demonstrated to be correlated with eitherCGOor the dependent measures. In model (4), I control forABS_UE, the absolute value ofUE, defined above.26I also include controls for firm size (Bamber (1987)), absolute announcement-window return (Karpoff (1987)), market-wide announcement-window turnover (Bamber, Barron, and Stober (1997)), closing stock price at the end of the fiscal quarter (Utama and Cready (1997)), average monthly share turnover for firmiover the prior 12 months, and momentum (Jegadeesh and Titman (1993), Grinblatt and Han (2005), Frazzini (2006)). I also include an indicator variable for NASDAQ firms, since trading volume is captured differently on NASDAQ.27
In model (5), the dependent measure controls for the Carhart (1997) risk factors, including momentum. I also control forUE, price (Bhushan (1994)), average turnover (Bhushan (1994)), return on assets (Balakrishnan, Bartov, and Faurel (2010), Fama and French (2015)), and losses (Hayn (1995)). In both models (2) and (3) I include year and Fama-French 10 industry classification indicators as controls, similar to Hirshleifer, Lim, and Teoh (2009). Prior to estimating regressions at the stock level, I rank all independent measures into deciles each quarter, and I scale decile rankings to have a mean of 0 and a range of 1.
TableIIpresents descriptive statistics for the variables in models (4) and (5), both for the full sample (N= 192,918) and separately for the unrealized gain (N= 100,097) and loss (N= 92,821) samples. The mean value ofAVOLTOTAL TRADES(0.510) in the full sample represents an increase in total trades of roughly 66.5% during the announcement window, relative to the median number of nonannouncement trades, while mean three-day abnormal returns are close to 0. With the exception ofBHAR(−1,1), the means of all of the variables presented in TableIIare significantly different across unrealized gain and loss observations (p< 0.01). Consistent with a univariate gain/loss disposition effect,AVOLis significantly higher for unrealized gain observations than for unrealized loss observations for all three measures presented. Further, the difference in mean abnormal volume is larger forAVOLSELLER-INIT TRADESthanAVOLBUYER-INIT TRADES.
TableIIIpresents Spearman correlations among key variables. Consistent with a disposition effect,CGOis positively correlated withAVOLSELLER-INIT TRADES(0.169). The slight positive correlation (0.005,p< 0.05) betweenCGOandBHAR(−1,1)is inconsistent with the predicted negative association. However, in subsequent tests, I find that, as predicted, a negative relation betweenCGOandBHAR(−1,1)emerges after I control forUE(and remains significant in analyses with multiple controls).CGOis significantly correlated with a number of the control variables, but no correlation is so high as to make multivariate analysis unfeasible. Intuitively,CGOis most highly correlated withPRICE(0.361) andMOMENTUM(0.518), which highlights the importance of controlling for these variables in multivariate analyses.
TableIVpresents coefficient estimates from ordinary least squares (OLS) regressions of model (4) usingAVOLSELLER-INIT TRADESas the dependent measure.t-Statistics reported in parentheses are calculated using standard errors two-way clustered by firm and event date (Hirshleifer, Lim, and Teoh (2009), Petersen (2009), Gow, Ormazabal, and Taylor (2010)). Column (1) presents results for the base model, column (2) examines the volatility and skewness predictions, column (3) considers the investor sophistication prediction, and column (4) estimates the full specification of model (4).
Consistent with an average disposition effect in abnormal seller-initiated volume, the coefficients onCGOare positive and significant (p< 0.01) across all specifications. Given that all interaction terms represent decile ranks with mean 0 and range of 1, the coefficients can be interpreted as the average marginal effect of moving from the lowest to the highestCGOdecile, evaluated at the means of the interacted covariates. Relative to the sample mean, the coefficient of 0.246 onCGOin column (1) implies that the marginal difference between the lowest and highestCGOdeciles is associated with an incremental difference in abnormal volume of approximately 40.6% of median nonannouncement trading.28
More importantly, with the exception ofCGO*ISKEW, all interaction terms in TableIVare significant in the predicted direction. Thus, TableIVpresents evidence that the disposition effect in abnormal seller-initiated volume increases with expected idiosyncratic return volatility, and decreases with investor sophistication and with the richness of the firm's information environment. The coefficient onCGO*ISKEWis significantly positive, which is inconsistent with the skewness prediction of Li and Yang (2013). As discussed further below, I find mixed results for the skewness prediction of Li and Yang (2013) throughout the analyses in my study. All control variables included in TableIVare significant in the predicted direction across all specifications, with the exception of an insignificant coefficient onAVG_TURNin column (4).
TableVpresents coefficient estimates from OLS regressions of model (5), whereBHAR(−1,1)is the dependent measure.t-Statistics reported in parentheses are calculated using standard errors two-way clustered by firm and event date (Hirshleifer, Lim, and Teoh (2009), Petersen (2009), Gow, Ormazabal, and Taylor (2010)). Columns (1) to (4) are organized as in TableIV. As predicted, the coefficient onCGOis significantly negative and approximately −0.015 across all four columns of TableV. This indicates that, ceteris paribus, three-day announcement-window abnormal returns are approximately −1.5% lower for observations in the highest quarterlyCGOdeciles than for observations in the lowest quarterlyCGOdeciles. These results are consistent with the prediction that an increased (restricted) supply of shares from stockholders who are in the gain (loss) portion of their preference function places downward (upward) announcement-window pressure on price.
The interaction results presented in TableVare also generally consistent with my predictions. As predicted, the coefficients onCGO*IVOLandCGO*ISKEWare negative and positive, respectively, in both columns (2) and (4). In column (3), the positive coefficient onCGO*FOLLOWINGis consistent with the investor sophistication prediction, while the coefficient onCGO*IORis insignificant. In column (4), when the investor sophistication proxies are included in the model with the return characteristics, the coefficient onCGO*FOLLOWINGbecomes insignificant and the coefficient onCGO*IORbecomes marginally negative (−0.005,t-statistic 1.97,p< 0.05), contrary to predictions.29Thus, TableVprovides strong support for the return characteristic predictions and moderate support for the investor sophistication prediction.
article.IV. Stock-Level Tests of Whether Stockho:
Motivated by prospect theory, prior literature predicts that the announcement-window pricing effects demonstrated in SectionIIIcan lead to an underreaction to earnings news and, in turn, to subsequent price drift (Frazzini (2006), Li and Yang (2013)). The key idea is that, when the short-window price pressure documented above runs counter to earnings news, prices will be slow to fully reflect the new information. This occurs when earnings news and stockholders' CGO have the same sign. To see this, note that given efficient price discovery, prices should quickly adjust upwards following positive earnings news. However, if investors in a gain position are eager to sell their shares, this excess supply places downward pressure on price, impeding upwards price discovery. An equivalent argument leads to temporarily inflated prices when investors receive bad news while in a loss position, due to their reluctance to sell absent a price premium. Essentially, a preference-driven underreaction to earnings news predicts that when stockholders' CGO and earnings news are of the same sign, preference-driven marginal disposition effects generate a lower return sensitivity to earnings news during the announcement window, and a corresponding correction evidenced by increased postearnings announcement drift.
Frazzini (2006) tests only for the latter part of this prediction: the association between stockholders’ CGO and monthly postearnings announcement drift. In a more general study using the same logic, Grinblatt and Han (2005) predict that the disposition effect generates the momentum anomaly (Jegadeesh and Titman (1993)) and test for patterns in future weekly returns. Both studies rely on patterns in future returns toinferthat these return patterns are consistent with the disposition effect hindering short-window price discovery around news announcements. The scarcity of more direct evidence, such as evidence of the predicted initial short-window reduced return response to news, has led researchers to question whether stockholders’ preferences with respect to their unrealized returns do in fact contribute to capital market phenomena such as postearnings announcement drift (Bernard and Thomas (1990)) and return momentum (Jegadeesh and Titman (1993)).
Novy-Marx (2012) shows that a CGO measure, constructed as in Frazzini (2006) using mutual fund holding data, does not subsume the momentum effect. He argues that any improvement to monthly calendar-time momentum trading strategies from sorting on stockholders’ capital gains position likely arises from mitigating the tax-related effects of poor calendar-time portfolio performance during January. Extending the work of Ben-David and Hirshleifer (2012), An (2016) finds evidence of higher future returns among stocks with both large unrealized gain and large unrealized loss overhangs, and infers that this evidence is consistent with selling pressure from a V-shaped disposition effect. These results call into question the link between theunconditionaldisposition effect andunconditionalreturn momentum, and highlight the importance of examining investors’ marginal behavior in response to identified shocks when testing theoretical predictions about preference-driven return underreaction to news.
Figures4and5present results from independent quarterly quintile sorts onUEandCGO. In Panel A of each figure, bars depict mean abnormal returns byCGOquintile, within each quintile of unexpected earnings (UE). Lighter colored left-hand bars represent lower quintiles ofCGO, while darker colored right-hand bars represent higher quintiles ofCGO. Panel B of each figure tests differences in mean returns for low and highUEquintiles with the highest (positive) and lowest (negative) spreads in capital gains. Spread quintiles are extremeCGOquintiles with the same sign asUE(i.e., lowUE, lowCGOand highUE, highCGO). Negative spread quintiles are extremeCGOquintiles whereCGOis news-contrarian (i.e., lowUE, highCGOand highUE, lowCGO). Thus, spread quintiles correspond to high values ofCGOSPREADin model (6), and negative spread quintiles correspond to low values ofCGOSPREADin model (6).
Figure4examinesBHAR(−1,1). First, consistent with the results presented in SectionIII, Panel A shows a significant negative average relation betweenCGOandBHAR(−1,1)across four of the fiveUEquintiles. Consistent with an underreaction to positive earnings news, Panels A and B show that highUEquintiles with highCGO(spread quintiles) have lower announcement-window returns than highUEquintiles with lowCGO(negative spread quintiles) (difference of −0.64%,p< 0.01). For lowUEquintiles, underreaction should predict a positive difference between the spread and negative spread quintiles, but I find that this difference is insignificant and slightly negative at −0.20%. Thus, both the investor-level results in SectionIIand the stock-level results presented here suggest that, around extreme bad news events, investors’ preference functions may not affect their trading behavior in the manner predicted by a pure prospect-theoretic explanation. Instead, results are consistent with extreme bad news disciplining the regret-avoidance (pride-seeking) behavior of investors in a loss (gain) position. The overall netCGOSPREADhedge return of −0.43%, however, remains significant in the predicted direction at the 10% level, consistent with an average preference-related underreaction.
In Figure5, which examinesBHAR(2,61), predictions are reversed in the postannouncement window. Postearnings announcement drift is generally concentrated around extreme positive and negative news announcements (e.g., Frazzini (2006)). In this regard, both high and lowUEquintiles display significantly positive average relations betweenCGOandBHAR(2,61). However, consistent with the results from SectionIIand Figure4, interquartileCGOspreads are not statistically significant for lowUEannouncements. Examining bivariate hedge returns, the interquartile spread between high and low earnings surprise firms is 8.33% for observations in spread quintiles, but only 1.96% for negative spread quintiles. Consistent with an average underreaction to extreme earnings news, this positive difference of approximately 6.38% is significant at the 1% level.
TableVIpresents coefficient estimates from OLS regressions of model (6) among various sample partitions.t-Statistics reported in parentheses are calculated using standard errors two-way clustered by firm and event date (Hirshleifer, Lim, and Teoh (2009), Petersen (2009), Gow, Ormazabal, and Taylor (2010)). Panel A (B) presents results whereBHAR(−1,1)(BHAR(2,61)) is the dependent measure. As predicted, the interaction betweenCGOSPREAD*UEis significantly negative (−0.026,p< 0.01) in Panel A and significantly positive (0.058,p< 0.10) in Panel B. In Panel A, the average return sensitivity to earnings news (δ2) is 7.1%. Recall that all regression variables represent mean-zero, range-one quarterly decile sorts. Thus, the reduction in return sensitivity associated with increasing from the lowest to the highest decile ofCGOSPREADis equivalent to approximately 36.6% (0.026/0.071) of the average announcement-window return sensitivity to earnings news. Panels A and B also show that the underreaction to earnings news is more severe in two of the three dimensions predicted by Li and Yang (2013). For example, the postannouncement coefficient onCGOSPREAD*UEis significant only for low following, low institutional ownership, and high volatility partitions of the data.31Similar to the volume results in SectionIII, the results in TableVIare inconsistent with the skewness prediction from Li and Yang (2013). Overall, the results in this section, together with the investor-level results in SectionII, are consistent with investors’ preference-related behavior contributing to an average underreaction to extreme earnings news that is stronger around positive earnings surprises than negative earnings surprises.
article.V. Discussion:
In addition to the analyses tabulated in my study, I perform several tests to determine whether my findings are sensitive to my research design choices. Some of these tests have been mentioned or footnoted throughout the text; others are discussed here.
First, I examine whether my stock-level findings are sensitive to my use ofCGOas a proxy for the representative investor's unrealized capital gain/loss position in a given stock. In untabulated tests, I repeat all of my analyses using the Frazzini (2006) CGO proxy, which is based on quarterly institutional holdings data, for the subsample of firms with available data to compute the measure. All qualitative inferences from the paper remain unchanged, although the investor sophistication and earnings underreaction results are generally weaker using the Frazzini (2006) measure, due to the institutional ownership requirements for computing the measure.
Second, in SectionVof the Internet Appendix, I examine seasonal and time-series variation in my results due to tax-motivated trading. Consistent with prior research, I find that the marginal announcement-window disposition effect that I observe is weaker during times when tax incentives are stronger or more salient, consistent with the capital gains tax lock-in effect mitigating the disposition effect in abnormal trading volume (Blouin, Raedy, and Shackelford (2003), Ivković, Poterba, and Weisbenner (2005), Jin (2006)). To determine whether my results are robust to the January-performance explanation of Novy-Marx (2012) for CGO's ability to predict returns, I repeat all stock-level analyses after excluding observations near the turn of the year. I find that all inferences remain unchanged. I also mimic the design of Frazzini (2006) by using daily calendar-time regressions for the (2,61) postannouncement window, and I find results consistent with his. All calendar-time inferences also remain unchanged if January returns are deleted from the calendar-time sample.
Finally, in untabulated tests I examine the sensitivity of my results to alternative proxies for investors’ beliefs about the volatility and skewness of future dividend growth (Li and Yang (2013)). Following Gu and Wu (2003), I use proxies for earnings volatility and skewness instead of return volatility and skewness. I also examine the more complex expected idiosyncratic return skewness proxy from Boyer, Mitton, and Vorkink (2010). Similar to the results presented in the text, I find results consistent with my predictions across the alternative volatility measures, but I continue to find mixed results for my skewness predictions across all skewness proxies. Results are mixed both with respect to my various skewness predictions and across various skewness proxies for each individual prediction, which highlights the difficulty of selecting an empirical proxy for investors’ beliefs about the skewness of future dividend growth.
This paper presents empirical evidence from (a) the investor level, (b) a short three-day stock-level announcement window, and (c) a 60-trading-day stock-level postannouncement window that is consistent with stockholders’ preferences with respect to their unrealized returns moderating their trading response to financial disclosures. The results also demonstrate that investors’ preference-motivated behavior can affect asset prices by generating a return underreaction to earnings news. These results address questions in recent literature about the link between investors’ preferences and trading behaviors such as the disposition effect, as well as questions about the relation between the disposition effect and asset returns. My analysis demonstrates the importance of identifying and controlling for investors’ trading motivations, such as their belief shocks, when examining theoretical predictions about the role of investors’ preferences in their trading decisions.
My findings motivate future research that seeks to isolate the role of investors’ preferences in their trading decisions. For example, future research might examine whether results are similar when investors trade in response to liquidity shocks instead of belief shocks. A limitation of my study is that I am unable to verify whether my investor-level tests successfully isolate the manner in which stockholders’ unrealized returns moderate their trading response to a belief shock for investors with short trading horizons. Future research could overcome this limitation by incorporating data that clearly identify investors’ belief-motivated trading decisions, or by conducting tests in a controlled experimental setting. Research in an experimental setting would also be helpful in testing whether earnings news moderates investors’ emotional tendencies toward pride-seeking and regret-aversion, as appears to be the case from my results. More generally, my results provide strong evidence that investors’ beliefs and preferences interact to jointly determine their trading decisions, but experimental and/or neurobiological evidence would be helpful in isolating the psychological or biological mechanisms behind this interaction.
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael R. Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton
